content.title: Pramod - A novel handover scheme using torch nodes and adaptive measurement aggregation mechanism to improve QoS in high-speed railway communication
content.abstract_or_snippet: as the distance increases. 6 Conclusion and future work This paper presents the details of a novel scheme to improve QoS in a high-speed railway communication system. The proposed scheme involves the following key features: (1) torch nodes to effectively forecast target DeNBs, (2) adaptive measurement aggregation based on mobility and (3) distributed mobile relays with load bal- resource utilization. The proposed ancer scheme was simulated using Omnet?? tool, and the per- formance is analyzed in terms of handover latency, han- dover failure probability and communication interruption probability. The preliminary analysis shows that the DMR- TN scheme outperforms the single-antenna scheme and provides better performance with reduced handover failure for efﬁcient and communication interruption probability. The proposed scheme can be easily implemented in high-speed railways with reduced implementation overhead. In the future work, we plan to carryout further optimization by considering service-based parameters (e.g., voice, video, and data) to enhance handover efﬁciency and resource optimization. content.abstract_or_snippet: die Frage in den Vordergrund, wie Daten und Informationen sowie deren Austausch und Nutzung über den gesamten Produktle- benszyklus gewinnbringend genutzt werden können. In einer Ausarbeitung des VDMA und des Fraunhofer-Center für Maritime Logistik und Dienstleistungen CML wurden sechs Handlungsfelder identifiziert, in denen maritime Zuliefer- unternehmen Betriebsdaten von vernetzten Systemen nutzen können, um angebotene Aftersales-Services weiterzuent- wickeln oder gänzlich neue Dienstleistungen zu offerieren [18]. Die Handlungsfelder umfassen sowohl die Analyse von Betriebsdaten zur Unterstützung der Produktentwicklung, die Betriebsoptimierung und -steuerung, die Zustandsüberwa- chung und Ferndiagnose, als auch die Möglichkeit Entschei- dungs- oder Arbeitsunterstützungssysteme anzubieten. Auch eine Ausarbeitung des VDMA aus dem Jahre 2018 identi- fizierte Erfolgsfaktoren, die den o.g. Handlungsfeldern zuge- ordnet werden können [30]. Abbildung 5 zeigt anschaulich, in welchen Bereichen der höchste Handlungsbedarf verbunden mit der höchsten zukünftigen Bedeutung in den Themenfel- dern zu finden sind. Dies sind auf den ersten drei Plätzen die Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit, Reaktions- und Lieferzeiten sowie die Ausfallzeiten beim Kunden. Die Notwendigkeit der Weiterent- wicklung ist der Branche bewusst. Besonders im Bereich der Zustandsüberwachung und Fern- diagnose stellt die Nutzung von leistungsfähigen Datenüber- tragungssystemen eine zentrale Herausforderung dar. all_chunks.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,

Vehicle identification microsystem The vehicle identification microsystem is crucial for the wayside monitoring system as all of the measured parameters of the passing train are linked to each vehicle of it. For that reason, such a microsystem should have the ability to work reliably at any environment conditions. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. RFID information flow. Table 1. Rail-TAG transponder specifications. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf und konfigurieren sich selbständig. Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten. In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen, Verkehrsprognosen und Umfahrungsempfehlungen ableiten. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: 16, 20 24 of 34 feature model inspired by FaceNet is adopted to ensure consistent tracking by associating objects between different frames. Reference [145] solves the problem of object detection in severe weather conditions by fusing cameras, LiDAR and radar using an adaptive deep fusion architecture. Reference [146] reviews the latest technologies for multi-object detection (MOD) and multi-object tracking (MOT) using deep neural networks (DNNs) for camera, LiDAR and radar sensor fusion. Most autonomous driving systems rely on 3D perception because it can provide depth information and 3D structure information around the vehicle. The precise 3D point cloud data provided by LiDAR is fused with visual information to achieve 3D object detection and tracking. Reference [147] developed a new method called DeepFusions to enhance 3D object detection by fusing deep features from LiDAR and camera sensors. The method consists of two key methods (InverseAug and LearnableAlign), which can help camera images to be effectively aligned with LiDAR point clouds at marginal computational cost (i.e., only one cross-attention layer). Reference [148] proposed a novel camera–LiDAR fusion 3D MOT framework called CAMO-MOT, which uses both camera and LiDAR data and significantly reduces tracking failures caused by occlusion and false detection. Reference [149] proposed a joint multi-object detection and tracking (JMODT) system based on end-to-end camera–LiDAR fusion, which performs joint detection and tracking by using 2D and 3D measurements for parallel object detection and association. Reference [150] proposes a new simultaneous detection and tracking baseline algorithm MotionTrack with multimodal sensor input in an autonomous driving environment. Current advances in deep learning will lead to more powerful object detection and tracking models, and with the application of mobile edge computing [151], sensor data can be processed faster, reducing latency and improving real-time performance. 4.2. Positioning and Mapping Autonomous driving needs to accurately determine the position and orientation of the environment in which it is located, and create or update the map of the environment in real time. This allows the vehicle to identify and avoid surrounding obstacles, while achieving safe navigation. In addition, the positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: impact of inaccurate or unreliable visual data, especially in low-light conditions. 5. Conclusions and Future Research Suggestions This paper deeply analyzes the environmental perception technology of multi-sensor information fusion in autonomous driving, and reveals the key role of making full use of sensor complementarity in improving the performance of autonomous driving systems. This paper focuses on the methods of heterogeneous sensor fusion, including the integra- tion of lidar, camera, millimeter-wave radar, and GNSS/IMU, to effectively improve the World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 27 of 34 vehicle’s perception of the surrounding environment and enhance the accuracy, stability and robustness of the system. Through a review of relevant literature, this paper sum- marizes the advantages of these sensors in applications such as data acquisition, target detection and tracking, positioning and map construction, and explores the key methods of calibration and data fusion in multi-sensor information fusion. This study finds that multi-sensor information fusion technology plays a key role in environmental perception of autonomous driving. Fusion technology significantly improves the accuracy of target detection and tracking, enhances the reliability of positioning and map construction, and improves the effect of scene segmentation. In particular, the introduction of deep learning technology has opened up new ways for multi-sensor information fusion, and the per- ception performance has been significantly improved by learning complex data features and patterns. In addition, this paper also explores the application of the Kalman filter, Bayesian estimation, D-S evidence theory, and deep learning algorithm in multi-sensor information fusion, and analyzes their role in specific tasks such as target detection and tracking, positioning and mapping, and scene segmentation. Looking to the future, the development direction of autonomous driving technology is to optimize sensor fusion algorithms, thereby improving the ability of autonomous driving systems to cope with complex and dynamic environments and improving real-time decision-making under various driving conditions. Improving sensor performance, espe- cially performance assurance in bad weather and complex environments, will be the key to ensuring the safety and reliability of autonomous driving. The seamless integration of perception systems and vehicle control systems is essential for the safety of autonomous vehicles, especially in high-speed or high-risk driving scenarios. In addition, the integra- tion of edge computing and cloud computing can effectively solve the problem of limited on-board computing resources and achieve real-time and efficient data processing. The inte- gration of smart cities and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) can enhance the interaction and data exchange between autonomous

The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: as the distance increases. 6 Conclusion and future work This paper presents the details of a novel scheme to improve QoS in a high-speed railway communication system. The proposed scheme involves the following key features: (1) torch nodes to effectively forecast target DeNBs, (2) adaptive measurement aggregation based on mobility and (3) distributed mobile relays with load bal- resource utilization. The proposed ancer scheme was simulated using Omnet?? tool, and the per- formance is analyzed in terms of handover latency, han- dover failure probability and communication interruption probability. The preliminary analysis shows that the DMR- TN scheme outperforms the single-antenna scheme and provides better performance with reduced handover failure for efﬁcient and communication interruption probability. The proposed scheme can be easily implemented in high-speed railways with reduced implementation overhead. In the future work, we plan to carryout further optimization by considering service-based parameters (e.g., voice, video, and data) to enhance handover efﬁciency and resource optimization. When the train is grouped, the length of the train will be fixed covering the length of all the locomotives and carriages (or EMUs for the high-speed railway) [10,11]. In the practical operation, a normal train integrity state indicates that the relative speed of the HOT (Head-of-Train) and EOT (End-of-Train) is zero and the projected HOT-EOT interval equals to the nominal train length. When decoupling occurs, the train will be in an incomplete state, the thus the EOT will lose the traction force and de- celerate until the speed falls to zero. An abnormal integrity status will result in an increased HOT-EOT relative speed, leading to an increasing HOT-EOT distance. Therefore, the train integrity state can be identified by evaluating the HOT/EOT relevant state and the train length. 4.1. Principle of length-based train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap. Due to the existence of positioning error, especially the uncertainty of EOT positioning resulted by the constrained satellite visibility, the uncertainty of the derived train length has to be concerned [12]. As shown in Fig. 6, an uncertainty buffer has to be added to the estimated HOT and EOT position to form an enhanced “envelope” for the train integrity determination purpose. A conservative evaluation of the un- certainty ranges is necessary due to the safety assurance target, and thus several factors related to the positioning performance have to be investigated, including the measurement noise, state estimation error and the potential fault(s) and failure(s). With this consideration, the derived train length would be larger than truth, and thus the threshold for train integrity identification has to be effectively determined to avoid false alarm to the abnormal train integrity events. The uncertainties in HOT and EOT positioning results lead to the es- timation deviation of the train length. When the quality of GNSS posi- tioning degrades with the change of the observation condition, the de- viation of train length estimation would be large and thus the failure of accurate train integrity monitoring may occur. Therefore, the consistency evaluation of the HOT/EOT relative speed can be involved to consolidate the integrity status identification through multiple information resources. Considering the errors in position and speed measurements of the both ends, it would be necessary to constrain the errors through the filtering operation. It can be found that there are mainly two specific trends to the monitoring variables, including relative static and relative acceleration according to the HOT-EOT-based train length and relative speed estimation. Therefore, a sliding window strategy can be involved for mean filtering, and then the filtered data in the window can be fitted by LS-based linear fitting. According to the fitting results, the qualita- tive trend of the data in the sliding window can be recognized to ana- lyze the changing status of the HOT/EOT relative speed and the esti- mation deviation of the train length. 4.2. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: . Safe train envelope estimation For GNSS-based train positioning and train integrity monitoring, the uncertainty in state estimation has to be concerned to ensure the safety and efficiency of train operation by utilizing the integrity monitoring result. Under the moving block train control scheme, where the occu- pancy of the track segments is determined by the practical length of the train, it is necessary to add a reasonable margin to the raw HOT and EOT location to form a safe occupancy range [13]. The safe train position has to cover the most unfavorable conditions. With this consideration, the margin should be sufficiently large to realize a high possibility to protect the unsatisfied situations. However, an excessive margin will lead to unreasonable identification of track occupancy, which may greatly af- fect the operational efficiency. To realize the expected balance level between the safety and efficiency characteristics, a constrained safe margin has to be adopted on the basis of safety and rationality, so as to reduce the train tracing interval and improve the system efficiency. The essence of the safe train envelope is to realize the safety as- surance of train control by describing the reliability of train positioning. The safe train envelope extends the positioning output based on the evaluation of the uncertainty of positioning errors, and provides a safe overlay to the most possible position derived by the HOT/EOT positioning unit. The safe margin consists of the HPL by integrity monitoring result and the confidential interval of the positioning error. The HPL, which indicates the radius of a circle in the horizontal plane with its center being at the true position to describe the region assured to contain the indicated horizontal position, is usually regarded as the safety boundary of positioning result by GNSS. However, the existence of the integrity risk may lead to an unbounded position error that exceeds the HPL. Without the high-level referencing system during the dynamic operation, it is difficult to accurately evaluate the relia- bility of the safety boundary by HPL. In order to guarantee the cred- ibility of the safe margin for envelope evaluation, it is necessary to estimate the possible range of the positioning error by a specific error confidence interval strategy. Considering the track-constrained along- track operation of railway trains, the error confidence interval indicates a one-dimensional along-track interval that can be represented by the uncertainties in state prediction, measurement update and the trackmap-based location determination, which means (4) and where confidence interval at instant k, result, geographic information from the trackmap database. represent the lower and upper limits of the indicates the user measurement represents the prior denotes the prediction result, and

input.query:
Welche Voraussetzungen/Faktoren müssen für einen Transfer von Technologie Wayside-Sensorik (Streckenseitige Sensoren) in Bereich Rolling
Stock in Motion erfüllt sein ?
input.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf Technologietransfer innerhalb des Schienenverkehrs von streckenseitiger Sensorik zu Rolling Stock in Motion. Für die Recherche müssen technische Voraussetzungen, Integrationsfaktoren, Betriebs- und Sicherheitsanforderungen sowie bestehende Anwendungsfälle und Normen getrennt betrachtet werden. Da aktuelle Web-Suche deaktiviert ist, werden alle Suchaufträge auf die interne Wissensbasis fokussiert und nach Relevanz priorisiert.

research_aspects.description: Untersucht, welche physikalischen, messtechnischen und umgebungsbedingten Voraussetzungen erfüllt sein müssen, damit streckenseitige Sensorprinzipien unter fahrzeugseitigen In-Motion-Bedingungen zuverlässig funktionieren.
Betrachtet die Einbindung der Sensorik in Rolling-Stock-Systeme, einschließlich Energieversorgung, Bauraum, IT/OT-Schnittstellen, Datenkommunikation und Verarbeitung mobiler Messdaten.
Analysiert, welche regulatorischen, normativen und funktionalen Sicherheitsanforderungen für den Transfer in den fahrzeugseitigen Bahnbetrieb erfüllt werden müssen.
Vergleicht geeignete Use Cases, Detektionsziele und Leistungskennzahlen von Wayside- und Onboard-Sensorik, um zu bestimmen, wann und mit welchem Nutzen ein Transfer sinnvoll ist.
Bewertet Instandhaltungsnutzen, Skalierbarkeit, Lebenszykluskosten, Verfügbarkeit und organisatorische Voraussetzungen, die den erfolgreichen Einsatz im Rolling Stock beeinflussen.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.abstract_or_snippet: die Frage in den Vordergrund, wie Daten und Informationen sowie deren Austausch und Nutzung über den gesamten Produktle- benszyklus gewinnbringend genutzt werden können. In einer Ausarbeitung des VDMA und des Fraunhofer-Center für Maritime Logistik und Dienstleistungen CML wurden sechs Handlungsfelder identifiziert, in denen maritime Zuliefer- unternehmen Betriebsdaten von vernetzten Systemen nutzen können, um angebotene Aftersales-Services weiterzuent- wickeln oder gänzlich neue Dienstleistungen zu offerieren [18]. Die Handlungsfelder umfassen sowohl die Analyse von Betriebsdaten zur Unterstützung der Produktentwicklung, die Betriebsoptimierung und -steuerung, die Zustandsüberwa- chung und Ferndiagnose, als auch die Möglichkeit Entschei- dungs- oder Arbeitsunterstützungssysteme anzubieten. Auch eine Ausarbeitung des VDMA aus dem Jahre 2018 identi- fizierte Erfolgsfaktoren, die den o.g. Handlungsfeldern zuge- ordnet werden können [30]. Abbildung 5 zeigt anschaulich, in welchen Bereichen der höchste Handlungsbedarf verbunden mit der höchsten zukünftigen Bedeutung in den Themenfel- dern zu finden sind. Dies sind auf den ersten drei Plätzen die Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit, Reaktions- und Lieferzeiten sowie die Ausfallzeiten beim Kunden. Die Notwendigkeit der Weiterent- wicklung ist der Branche bewusst. Besonders im Bereich der Zustandsüberwachung und Fern- diagnose stellt die Nutzung von leistungsfähigen Datenüber- tragungssystemen eine zentrale Herausforderung dar. content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater

Taking local conditions and the remoteness of a check-point, there are basically two methods for transferring data – by the fiber-optic network of NRIC (wired) or by the GSM-R technology (wirelessly). The locations of the check-points throughout the railway in Bulgaria are shown on Fig. 1. into account the in Bulgaria will feature For reasons of economy and efficiency the wayside monitoring system the following functions: 1) Identification of a vehicle by its individual number; 2) Measuring the load of each wheel – detection of overload, unbalanced vehicles and uneven load distribution between the wheels and axles; 3) Detection of wheel defects – flat spots, out of roundness, polygonizaition, etc.; 4) Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes; 5) Telecommunication between the local check-points and control centers. Fig. 1. Map of the locations of the check-points throughout the railway in Bulgaria. 3. SENSORS AND MICROSYSTEMS to needs Each of the check-points (Fig. 1) has been individually equipped with sensors and microsystems, depending on its designed features. Basically every vehicle check-point identification and telecommunication. But these two features alone could not deliver useful information about some of the parameters of the passing trains. This demands at least one more feature to be added, e.g. detection of wheel load, detection of wheel defects or detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes. featuring be 3.1. Vehicle identification microsystem The vehicle identification microsystem is crucial for the wayside monitoring system as all of the measured parameters of the passing train are linked to each vehicle of it. For that reason, such a microsystem should have the ability to work reliably at any environment conditions. The microsystem should also be able to accurately read the vehicle’s identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. During the decision making about what type of identification method should the microsystems is best to use, the research team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf und konfigurieren sich selbständig. Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten. In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen, Verkehrsprognosen und Umfahrungsempfehlungen ableiten. Als Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN, deutsch: drahtloses lokales Netzwerk) wird ein lokales Funknetz und dessen verschiedene Techniken und

content.abstract_or_snippet: 16, 20 24 of 34 feature model inspired by FaceNet is adopted to ensure consistent tracking by associating objects between different frames. Reference [145] solves the problem of object detection in severe weather conditions by fusing cameras, LiDAR and radar using an adaptive deep fusion architecture. Reference [146] reviews the latest technologies for multi-object detection (MOD) and multi-object tracking (MOT) using deep neural networks (DNNs) for camera, LiDAR and radar sensor fusion. Most autonomous driving systems rely on 3D perception because it can provide depth information and 3D structure information around the vehicle. The precise 3D point cloud data provided by LiDAR is fused with visual information to achieve 3D object detection and tracking. Reference [147] developed a new method called DeepFusions to enhance 3D object detection by fusing deep features from LiDAR and camera sensors. The method consists of two key methods (InverseAug and LearnableAlign), which can help camera images to be effectively aligned with LiDAR point clouds at marginal computational cost (i.e., only one cross-attention layer). Reference [148] proposed a novel camera–LiDAR fusion 3D MOT framework called CAMO-MOT, which uses both camera and LiDAR data and significantly reduces tracking failures caused by occlusion and false detection. Reference [149] proposed a joint multi-object detection and tracking (JMODT) system based on end-to-end camera–LiDAR fusion, which performs joint detection and tracking by using 2D and 3D measurements for parallel object detection and association. Reference [150] proposes a new simultaneous detection and tracking baseline algorithm MotionTrack with multimodal sensor input in an autonomous driving environment. Current advances in deep learning will lead to more powerful object detection and tracking models, and with the application of mobile edge computing [151], sensor data can be processed faster, reducing latency and improving real-time performance. 4.2. Positioning and Mapping Autonomous driving needs to accurately determine the position and orientation of the environment in which it is located, and create or update the map of the environment in real time. This allows the vehicle to identify and avoid surrounding obstacles, while achieving safe navigation. In addition, the positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: impact of inaccurate or unreliable visual data, especially in low-light conditions. 5. Conclusions and Future Research Suggestions This paper deeply analyzes the environmental perception technology of multi-sensor information fusion in autonomous driving, and reveals the key role of making full use of sensor complementarity in improving the performance of autonomous driving systems. This paper focuses on the methods of heterogeneous sensor fusion, including the integra- tion of lidar, camera, millimeter-wave radar, and GNSS/IMU, to effectively improve the World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 27 of 34 vehicle’s perception of the surrounding environment and enhance the accuracy, stability and robustness of the system. Through a review of relevant literature, this paper sum- marizes the advantages of these sensors in applications such as data acquisition, target detection and tracking, positioning and map construction, and explores the key methods of calibration and data fusion in multi-sensor information fusion. This study finds that multi-sensor information fusion technology plays a key role in environmental perception of autonomous driving. Fusion technology significantly improves the accuracy of target detection and tracking, enhances the reliability of positioning and map construction, and improves the effect of scene segmentation. In particular, the introduction of deep learning technology has opened up new ways for multi-sensor information fusion, and the per- ception performance has been significantly improved by learning complex data features and patterns. In addition, this paper also explores the application of the Kalman filter, Bayesian estimation, D-S evidence theory, and deep learning algorithm in multi-sensor information fusion, and analyzes their role in specific tasks such as target detection and tracking, positioning and mapping, and scene segmentation. Looking to the future, the development direction of autonomous driving technology is to optimize sensor fusion algorithms, thereby improving the ability of autonomous driving systems to cope with complex and dynamic environments and improving real-time decision-making under various driving conditions. Improving sensor performance, espe- cially performance assurance in bad weather and complex environments, will be the key to ensuring the safety and reliability of autonomous driving. The seamless integration of perception systems and vehicle control systems is essential for the safety of autonomous vehicles, especially in high-speed or high-risk driving scenarios. In addition, the integra- tion of edge computing and cloud computing can effectively solve the problem of limited on-board computing resources and achieve real-time and efficient data processing. content.title: Pramod - A novel handover scheme using torch nodes and adaptive measurement aggregation mechanism to improve QoS in high-speed railway communication
content.abstract_or_snippet: as the distance increases. 6 Conclusion and future work This paper presents the details of a novel scheme to improve QoS in a high-speed railway communication system. The proposed scheme involves the following key features: (1) torch nodes to effectively forecast target DeNBs, (2) adaptive measurement aggregation based on mobility and (3) distributed mobile relays with load bal- resource utilization. The proposed ancer scheme was simulated using Omnet?? tool, and the per- formance is analyzed in terms of handover latency, han- dover failure probability and communication interruption probability. The preliminary analysis shows that the DMR- TN scheme outperforms the single-antenna scheme and provides better performance with reduced handover failure for efﬁcient and communication interruption probability. The proposed scheme can be easily implemented in high-speed railways with reduced implementation overhead. In the future work, we plan to carryout further optimization by considering service-based parameters (e.g., voice, video, and data) to enhance handover efﬁciency and resource optimization. metadata.task_rationale: Bewertet, welche Daten- und Systemarchitekturen für den Transfer in bewegte Fahrzeuge notwendig sind.

domain_applicability.reason: Handover-, QoS- und Relay-Mechanismen für Hochgeschwindigkeitskommunikation sind auch in Bahnkontext relevant.

hypotheses.statement: Ein Transfer von Wayside-Sensorprinzipien in Rolling Stock in Motion ist nur dann technisch tragfähig, wenn das jeweilige Messprinzip unter dynamischen Fahrbedingungen dieselbe Zielgröße ohne streckenseitige Referenz zuverlässig erfassen kann; besonders kritisch sind Geschwindigkeit, Witterung, Verschmutzung und die veränderte mechanische Ankopplung des Sensors.
hypotheses.reasoning: Das Wayside-System adressiert sicherheitskritische Größen wie Hot Boxes, Bremsen, Raddynamik und Lastverteilung unter Vorbeifahrtbedingungen. Die Dokumente zeigen zugleich, dass Performance stark von Umwelt- und Betriebsbedingungen abhängt: OCR scheiterte an Schmutz, Witterung und Zuggeschwindigkeit; Beschleunigungs- und IR-Messung sind an definierte Einbauorte und Triggerlogik gekoppelt. Für einen Onboard-Transfer müssen diese Einflussgrößen beherrscht bzw. kompensiert werden.

hypotheses.statement: Für den Transfer ist eine fahrzeugseitige Systemintegration mit eindeutiger Objekt-/Achszurodnung, energiegesicherter Sensorik und robuster Datenarchitektur erforderlich; Funktionen wie Triggering, Zeit-/Achszählbezug, lokale Vorverarbeitung und standardisierte Übertragung müssen onboard äquivalent umgesetzt werden.
hypotheses.reasoning: Im Wayside-System sind Identifikation, Rail-Contact-Triggering, Achszählung, Messzonenlogik, lokale Datenverarbeitung, XML-basierte Übertragung und Backup-Stromversorgung integrale Bestandteile der Messfunktion. Daraus folgt, dass ein Transfer auf Rolling Stock nicht nur den Sensor selbst, sondern auch diese Systemfunktionen in fahrzeugseitiger Form abbilden muss, damit Messdaten korrekt zugeordnet, verarbeitet und übermittelt werden können.

hypotheses.statement: Ein sinnvoller Transfer ist vor allem für solche Use Cases zu erwarten, bei denen der betriebliche Nutzen aus kontinuierlicher Zustandsüberwachung, Alarmierung und Instandhaltungsplanung die höhere Integrationskomplexität übersteigt; prioritäre Kandidaten sind Temperaturüberwachung, Raddiagnostik und Last-/Unwuchtbewertung.
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Wayside-Dokumente nennen als Hauptnutzen Unfallvermeidung, geringere Fahrzeug- und Infrastrukturkosten, bessere Wartungsplanung, Echtzeit-Tracking und Erkennung von Überladung bzw. Lastverteilung. Diese Nutzenargumente definieren zugleich die erfolgversprechendsten Transferfelder. Ein Onboard-Transfer ist daher besonders dort plausibel, wo häufige oder kontinuierliche Zustandsdaten einen zusätzlichen Instandhaltungs- und Verfügbarkeitswert liefern.

hypotheses.statement: Auf Basis der vorliegenden Dokumente lässt sich für Sicherheits-, Zulassungs- und Normenanforderungen nur die Hypothese stützen, dass ein Transfer mindestens die bereits im Wayside-System genutzten interoperabilitäts- und schnittstellenbezogenen Vorgaben für Identifikation und Kommunikation berücksichtigen muss; belastbare Aussagen zu funktionaler Sicherheit oder Zulassungsnormen fehlen in der Evidenz.
hypotheses.reasoning: Explizit genannt werden TSI-bezogene Montage/Lage des Rail-TAG sowie Kommunikationsprotokolle und Schnittstellen (XML, LAN, GSM-R/UMTS, Web/FTP/TelNet, SMS-Alarme). Konkrete Aussagen zu EN 50126/8/9, SIL oder Zulassungsverfahren enthalten die bereitgestellten Dokumente jedoch nicht. Daher ist nur eine eingeschränkte, prüfbare Minimalhypothese möglich.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.title: Studie Digitaler Zwilling Schiffbau Schiffsbetrieb 2023
content.abstract_or_snippet: die Frage in den Vordergrund, wie Daten und Informationen sowie deren Austausch und Nutzung über den gesamten Produktle- benszyklus gewinnbringend genutzt werden können. In einer Ausarbeitung des VDMA und des Fraunhofer-Center für Maritime Logistik und Dienstleistungen CML wurden sechs Handlungsfelder identifiziert, in denen maritime Zuliefer- unternehmen Betriebsdaten von vernetzten Systemen nutzen können, um angebotene Aftersales-Services weiterzuent- wickeln oder gänzlich neue Dienstleistungen zu offerieren [18]. Die Handlungsfelder umfassen sowohl die Analyse von Betriebsdaten zur Unterstützung der Produktentwicklung, die Betriebsoptimierung und -steuerung, die Zustandsüberwa- chung und Ferndiagnose, als auch die Möglichkeit Entschei- dungs- oder Arbeitsunterstützungssysteme anzubieten. Auch eine Ausarbeitung des VDMA aus dem Jahre 2018 identi- fizierte Erfolgsfaktoren, die den o.g. Handlungsfeldern zuge- ordnet werden können [30]. Abbildung 5 zeigt anschaulich, in welchen Bereichen der höchste Handlungsbedarf verbunden mit der höchsten zukünftigen Bedeutung in den Themenfel- dern zu finden sind. Dies sind auf den ersten drei Plätzen die Ersatzteilverfügbarkeit, Reaktions- und Lieferzeiten sowie die Ausfallzeiten beim Kunden. Die Notwendigkeit der Weiterent- wicklung ist der Branche bewusst. Besonders im Bereich der Zustandsüberwachung und Fern- diagnose stellt die Nutzung von leistungsfähigen Datenüber- tragungssystemen eine zentrale Herausforderung dar. content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf und konfigurieren sich selbständig. Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten. In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen, Verkehrsprognosen und Umfahrungsempfehlungen ableiten. content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: 16, 20 24 of 34 feature model inspired by FaceNet is adopted to ensure consistent tracking by associating objects between different frames. Reference [145] solves the problem of object detection in severe weather conditions by fusing cameras, LiDAR and radar using an adaptive deep fusion architecture. Reference [146] reviews the latest technologies for multi-object detection (MOD) and multi-object tracking (MOT) using deep neural networks (DNNs) for camera, LiDAR and radar sensor fusion. Most autonomous driving systems rely on 3D perception because it can provide depth information and 3D structure information around the vehicle. The precise 3D point cloud data provided by LiDAR is fused with visual information to achieve 3D object detection and tracking. Reference [147] developed a new method called DeepFusions to enhance 3D object detection by fusing deep features from LiDAR and camera sensors. The method consists of two key methods (InverseAug and LearnableAlign), which can help camera images to be effectively aligned with LiDAR point clouds at marginal computational cost (i.e., only one cross-attention layer). Reference [148] proposed a novel camera–LiDAR fusion 3D MOT framework called CAMO-MOT, which uses both camera and LiDAR data and significantly reduces tracking failures caused by occlusion and false detection. Reference [149] proposed a joint multi-object detection and tracking (JMODT) system based on end-to-end camera–LiDAR fusion, which performs joint detection and tracking by using 2D and 3D measurements for parallel object detection and association. Reference [150] proposes a new simultaneous detection and tracking baseline algorithm MotionTrack with multimodal sensor input in an autonomous driving environment. Current advances in deep learning will lead to more powerful object detection and tracking models, and with the application of mobile edge computing [151], sensor data can be processed faster, reducing latency and improving real-time performance. 4.2. Positioning and Mapping Autonomous driving needs to accurately determine the position and orientation of the environment in which it is located, and create or update the map of the environment in real time. This allows the vehicle to identify and avoid surrounding obstacles, while achieving safe navigation. In addition, the positioning system should be sufficiently robust and accurate to cope with various complex environments and severe weather conditions. Generally, in autonomous driving systems, it is a common practice to increase the overall positioning and mapping performance by fusing two or more sensors. Reference [152

content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: impact of inaccurate or unreliable visual data, especially in low-light conditions. 5. Conclusions and Future Research Suggestions This paper deeply analyzes the environmental perception technology of multi-sensor information fusion in autonomous driving, and reveals the key role of making full use of sensor complementarity in improving the performance of autonomous driving systems. This paper focuses on the methods of heterogeneous sensor fusion, including the integra- tion of lidar, camera, millimeter-wave radar, and GNSS/IMU, to effectively improve the World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 27 of 34 vehicle’s perception of the surrounding environment and enhance the accuracy, stability and robustness of the system. Through a review of relevant literature, this paper sum- marizes the advantages of these sensors in applications such as data acquisition, target detection and tracking, positioning and map construction, and explores the key methods of calibration and data fusion in multi-sensor information fusion. This study finds that multi-sensor information fusion technology plays a key role in environmental perception of autonomous driving. Fusion technology significantly improves the accuracy of target detection and tracking, enhances the reliability of positioning and map construction, and improves the effect of scene segmentation. In particular, the introduction of deep learning technology has opened up new ways for multi-sensor information fusion, and the per- ception performance has been significantly improved by learning complex data features and patterns. In addition, this paper also explores the application of the Kalman filter, Bayesian estimation, D-S evidence theory, and deep learning algorithm in multi-sensor information fusion, and analyzes their role in specific tasks such as target detection and tracking, positioning and mapping, and scene segmentation. Looking to the future, the development direction of autonomous driving technology is to optimize sensor fusion algorithms, thereby improving the ability of autonomous driving systems to cope with complex and dynamic environments and improving real-time decision-making under various driving conditions. Improving sensor performance, espe- cially performance assurance in bad weather and complex environments, will be the key to ensuring the safety and reliability of autonomous driving. The seamless integration of perception systems and vehicle control systems is essential for the safety of autonomous vehicles, especially in high-speed or high-risk driving scenarios. In addition, the integra- tion of edge computing and cloud computing can effectively solve the problem of limited on-board computing resources and achieve real-time and efficient data processing. The inte- gration of smart cities and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) can enhance the interaction and data exchange between autonomous

content.title: Pramod - A novel handover scheme using torch nodes and adaptive measurement aggregation mechanism to improve QoS in high-speed railway communication
content.abstract_or_snippet: as the distance increases. 6 Conclusion and future work This paper presents the details of a novel scheme to improve QoS in a high-speed railway communication system. The proposed scheme involves the following key features: (1) torch nodes to effectively forecast target DeNBs, (2) adaptive measurement aggregation based on mobility and (3) distributed mobile relays with load bal- resource utilization. The proposed ancer scheme was simulated using Omnet?? tool, and the per- formance is analyzed in terms of handover latency, han- dover failure probability and communication interruption probability. The preliminary analysis shows that the DMR- TN scheme outperforms the single-antenna scheme and provides better performance with reduced handover failure for efﬁcient and communication interruption probability. The proposed scheme can be easily implemented in high-speed railways with reduced implementation overhead. In the future work, we plan to carryout further optimization by considering service-based parameters (e.g., voice, video, and data) to enhance handover efﬁciency and resource optimization. updated_hypotheses.statement: Ein Transfer von Wayside-Sensorprinzipien in Rolling Stock in Motion ist nur dann technisch tragfähig, wenn das jeweilige Messprinzip unter dynamischen Fahrbedingungen dieselbe Zielgröße ohne streckenseitige Referenz zuverlässig erfassen kann; besonders kritisch sind Geschwindigkeit, Witterung, Verschmutzung und die veränderte mechanische Ankopplung des Sensors.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese passt gut zur Evidenz: Die Wayside-Dokumente zeigen, dass die Messung stark an konkrete Betriebsbedingungen und die mechanische Einbindung gebunden ist. OCR scheiterte explizit an Geschwindigkeit, Verschmutzung und Witterung; Beschleunigungs- und IR-Messungen sind an definierte Einbauorte sowie Triggerlogik gekoppelt. Das stützt die Aussage, dass ein Onboard-Transfer nur tragfähig ist, wenn das Messprinzip die Zielgröße unter dynamischen Bedingungen ohne streckenseitige Referenz ebenso zuverlässig erfasst. Ein direkter Gegenbeleg in den vorliegenden Dokumenten fehlt.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Für den Transfer ist eine fahrzeugseitige Systemintegration mit eindeutiger Objekt-/Achszurodnung, energiegesicherter Sensorik und robuster Datenarchitektur erforderlich; Funktionen wie Triggering, Zeit-/Achszählbezug, lokale Vorverarbeitung und standardisierte Übertragung müssen onboard äquivalent umgesetzt werden.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Evidenz stützt diese Hypothese sehr gut. Die Wayside-Architektur umfasst Identifikation, Triggering über Rail Contacts, Achszählung, lokale Datenverarbeitung, XML-basierte Datenreduktion/-übertragung, Kommunikationsschnittstellen und Energieversorgung/Backup als integrale Bestandteile der Messfunktion. Daraus folgt mit hoher Plausibilität, dass ein Transfer in Rolling Stock in Motion fahrzeugseitige Äquivalente für Objekt-/Achszurodnung, Triggering, Vorverarbeitung und robuste Übertragung erfordert. Es gibt keine belastbaren Dokumente, die eine reine Sensorsubstitution ohne diese Systemfunktionen nahelegen.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Ein sinnvoller Transfer ist vor allem für solche Use Cases zu erwarten, bei denen der betriebliche Nutzen aus kontinuierlicher Zustandsüberwachung, Alarmierung und Instandhaltungsplanung die höhere Integrationskomplexität übersteigt; prioritäre Kandidaten sind Temperaturüberwachung, Raddiagnostik und Last-/Unwuchtbewertung.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese ist semantisch plausibel, aber nur mittel gestützt. Die Dokumente belegen den Nutzen von Zustandsüberwachung, Alarmierung, Echtzeit-Tracking und Wartungsplanung sowie konkrete Wayside-Use-Cases wie Temperatur, Raddiagnostik und Lastverteilung. Nicht direkt belegt ist jedoch der Vergleich, dass dieser Nutzen die höhere Integrationskomplexität eines Onboard-Transfers übersteigt. Dafür fehlt eine breitere Evidenzbasis zu Aufwand-Nutzen-Abwägungen im Rolling-Stock-Kontext. Ein Falsifikationsversuch war mangels direkter Onboard-Vergleichsdaten nur eingeschränkt möglich.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Auf Basis der vorliegenden Dokumente lässt sich für Sicherheits-, Zulassungs- und Normenanforderungen nur die Hypothese stützen, dass ein Transfer mindestens die bereits im Wayside-System genutzten interoperabilitäts- und schnittstellenbezogenen Vorgaben für Identifikation und Kommunikation berücksichtigen muss; belastbare Aussagen zu funktionaler Sicherheit oder Zulassungsnormen fehlen in der Evidenz.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Die Hypothese ist vorsichtig formuliert und passt gut zur Evidenzlage. Die Dokumente nennen interoperabilitäts- und schnittstellenbezogene Anforderungen wie TSI-bezogene Rail-TAG-Montage/Lage sowie Kommunikationsprotokolle und Interfaces (XML, LAN, GSM-R/UMTS, Web/FTP/TelNet, SMS-Alarme). Zugleich enthalten die bereitgestellten Quellen keine belastbaren Aussagen zu funktionaler Sicherheit, SIL oder Zulassungsnormen. Damit wird genau die Minimalhypothese gestützt, dass mindestens diese Kommunikations-/Identifikationsvorgaben zu berücksichtigen sind, weitergehende Sicherheitsnorm-Aussagen aber nicht evidenzbasiert getroffen werden können.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

challenge_findings.description: Die Evidenz zeigt klare Randbedingungen der Messprinzipien: Identifikation per OCR scheitert bei Schmutz, Witterung und Geschwindigkeit; andere Messungen sind an definierte Sensorpositionen und Triggermechanismen gekoppelt. Das unterstützt die Notwendigkeit robuster Erfassung unter veränderten dynamischen und mechanischen Bedingungen bei einem Transfer.

Die Wayside-Systembeschreibung belegt, dass Messfunktion und Systemintegration untrennbar verbunden sind: Identifikation, Achszählung, Triggering, lokale Verarbeitung und standardisierte Übertragung sind funktionale Voraussetzungen, nicht bloß Zusatzmerkmale.

Die Dokumente belegen Nutzenpotenziale und prioritäre Wayside-Anwendungsfelder, liefern aber keine direkte Evidenz zur ökonomischen oder technischen Schwelle, ab der ein Onboard-Transfer die höhere Integrationskomplexität rechtfertigt.

Die vorliegenden Quellen erlauben nur Aussagen zu Identifikations- und Kommunikationsschnittstellen sowie TSI-bezogener Rail-TAG-Montage; explizite Evidenz zu funktionaler Sicherheit, SIL, Zulassungsverfahren oder einschlägigen EN-Normen fehlt.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion.pdf
